reason: The text contains multiple named characters engaged in dialogue with clear professions and roles. Specific examples include: (1) Shiphrah and Puah (שִׁפְרָה and פּוּעָֽה), named midwives who have dialogue with Pharaoh; (2) Pharaoh himself, who speaks to his people (וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אֶל־ עַמּ֑וֹ) with specific directives; (3) Pharaoh's daughter (בַּת־ פַּרְעֹה), who engages in dialogue with her servants and Moses's sister (וַתֹּ֕אמֶר); (4) A man from the house of Levi (אִ֖ישׁ מִבֵּ֣ית לֵוִ֑י) and a woman (הָאִשָּׁ֖ה) described as parents; (5) Moses's sister (אֲחֹתוֹ), who speaks to Pharaoh's daughter (וַתֹּ֣אמֶר אֲחֹתוֹ); and (6) Moses himself (מֹשֶׁ֔ה), named after being rescued. These are all regular people with names, professions/roles, and documented dialogue.
